"The Earth flowers and bears manifold colored blooms and fruits, and in her interior has grown a large tree with a silver trunk, stretching itself out to the Earth's surface. The image is the sixth illustration from Splendor Solis. It shows two philosophers standing beside a tree while a peasant gathers fruit from it.  Below is a scene of women in a public bath.

comments.title: Regarding classical music and occultism
comments.content:
How is classical music perceived from a Western occultist standpoint? Is it more conducive to spiritual endeavours than folk music and other types of music? I ask because the experiments performed by Dorothy Retallack (as given in Tompkins' book) clearly showed that plants are fond of classical music (both Western and Hindustani) as opposed to other types of music like rock music, for example. That's why I thought that maybe there could be an occult dimension to this. Could you help me out?

Regards,
Viduraawakened

replies.title: Re: Regarding classical music and occultism
replies.content: Most old-fashioned occult writers think very highly of Western classical music. (Most of them lived before you could find out much about classical Hindustani music in the West, so they didn't comment on it.) There's an extensive literature on the subject -- the writings of Joscelyn Godwin on the occult dimensions of music are a great place to start here. Tastes vary; most classic occult authors I know of thought very highly of Baroque masters such as Bach, Handel, and Mozart; opinions were divided about most of the later Romantic composers -- though in the late 19th and early 20th century, just about everyone in the occult scene was crazy about Wagner's operas!

replies.title: Re: Regarding classical music and occultism
replies.content: Thank you for your reply. That does sound interesting. I don't know much about Western Classical Music to make an educated and informed comment, but I have listened to certain recordings of Chopin and Tchaikovsky online and found them really beautiful. It makes sense why the old school authors of occultism would like this music. While it is fundamentally different from Hindustani Classical Music, it is really beautiful in its own way. From what little I know of Western Classical Music, it evolved from the hymns played and sung in churches across Western Europe (please feel free to correct me if I'm mistaken), so it's not really surprising that practicing occultists would have respect for it.

comments.title: Great Conjunction
comments.content:
Thanks to Astrodienst I've determined that in my time zone tomorrow's great conjunction perfects down to a single second of arc at 10:18 AM. At my location, Venus is applying to conjoin midheaven, within two degrees, as well as being in Saggitarius, my rising sign, trine the Moon and unafflicted, so far as I can tell. Is there any way that I can use this seemingly rather favorable situation of Venus to my advantage, seeing as this conjuction is so important and liable to influence events for the next couple of centuries?

I infer that Jupiter catches up with Saturn every twelve to fourteen years or thereabouts, so they must conjoin to some degree about that often. What features in particular make this conjunction so important? Is it that they are so close as to appear to form a single body?

replies.title: Re: Great Conjunction
replies.content:
Jupiter and Saturn are conjunct every 20 years. What makes the conjunction tomorrow so important is that the conjunctions of Jupiter and Saturn occur in signs of one element for between 150 and 200 years, and when they shift into a different element that marks a new era in world history. The era ending tomorrow began in 1842, and the era that begins tomorrow will end in 2219. Wednesday's post on the blog will discuss all this in detail, and give charts for the 1843, 2020, and 2219 conjunctions, as well as the one in 1226, the last time we had a shift from Earth to Air.

As for what you can do about it, that depends on your magical chops. What kind of magical training and experience do you have? Since Venus isn't in aspect to the conjunction, you could always do an ordinary Venus working, to consecrate a talisman or what have you; if you want to do more than that, you need to have some hefty magical skills.

replies.title: Re: Great Conjunction
replies.content: I find it very interesting also that before 1226, the last shift from Earth to Air was in 450 AD. Anyone familiar with the 5th Century will find that rather striking.

replies.title: Re: Great Conjunction
replies.content: Very much so. Air eras are always periods of the dissolution of old forms and the collapse of rigidly defended certainties...

replies.title: Re: Great Conjunction
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Great Mutation
replies.content:
I looked up what happened in 1842, and these things caught my eye.

James Nasmyth patents the steam hammer in the United Kingdom.
Queen Victoria becomes the first reigning British monarch to travel by train, on the Great Western Railway between Slough and London Paddington station.
The Income Tax Act establishes the first peacetime income tax in the United Kingdom; 7 pence in the pound, for incomes over 150 pounds.
Delft University of Technology is established by William II of the Netherlands, as a 'Royal Academy for the education of civilian engineers'.
The Massachusetts Supreme Court makes strikes and unions legal in the United States.
The Mines Act 1842 becomes law, prohibiting underground work for all women and boys under 10 years old in the United Kingdom.

It seems that this is when full scale industrialisation and huge technological advances started occurring. Also laws were enacted to deal with the problems caused by said industrialisation. So, are we going to see de-industrialisation now and a shift to more human and small scale endeavours? Along with a new mentality to deal with the unravelling of what most people currently believe in? The end of globalisation? (hopefully)

comments.title: Mum's transition
comments.content:
Hi JMG and readers, Ive mentioned previously about my mum's stroke back in February last year, which basically killed her short term memory. She had also begun to show signs of dementia prior to that event.
From that time on I feel that I was in mourning for my mum, although all of us, in particular my sister and I did whatever we could to try and make her life as good as possible, a somewhat exhausting and very repetitive task.
Due to a 2nd covid lockdown about a month ago, we brought her home just before it was enforced.
I started to notice behaviour that seemed to be preparing for leaving.
On Tuesday morning with a full plate of bacon and eggs in front of her, a request she gave to my sister who was staying there to help look after her, my sister heard a noise and came out of the kitchen to see mum leaning to one side in her chair.
I got a frantic call from my brother and arrived before the ambulance to find mum on the floor and my brother attempting heart massage while my sister held the phone with a person giving instructions.
I immediately said leave her, let her go it's her time.
I knelt down and said goodbye to mum and it felt such a release.
I was thinking of what you had said JMG about her mental sheath breaking down and her journey being an easier one because of that.
Have I got that right?
Knowing that and that after over 2 years of suffering - other health problems prior to the stroke - and a quick, and I would imagine, fairly painless death, I didn't feel any grief just gladness for her that she was on her way to the next phase.
When the ambulance officers arrived and we said don't attempt anything, they ascertained that she was gone and put her back in her bed.
A family friend managed to get her (now retired Catholic) priest to come out, within a couple of hours and he annointed and blessed her.
Our small family group all arrived at about this time and everyone kissed mum and said goodbye.
She stayed with us all day, with a small candle burning in the room, and we would come and go sitting with her, including Dad.
It all felt so normal and nice, for everyone really. I would never have thought that sitting in the bedroom with my dead mother would be something I would experience, and feel so calm about, but I'm so grateful that we were all able to do so.
She had a less than ideal end stage to her life, but she had a real good death.
Do you think JMG, reading this fairly bare bones account, you would say she's had a good transition?

Her funeral is this Wednesday, with a full Catholic mass, not that any of us besides Dad and maybe my other sister still keep the faith so to speak. But we respect her wishes.

Helen in Oz

PS she was 91

I have a couple of questions related to affirmations. Apologies if this information has already been covered earlier.

For about six months or so, I have been using the affirmation "I am an accomplished ABC practitioner" (ABC is a placeholder). There have been a couple of events that make me think that the affirmation has already worked (TSW!), but I can't help feeling that the affirmation was quite open-ended, so I have decided to change it to "I am a practitioner who ... comments.title: the dolmen arch
comments.content: Good morning and Happy Holidays... I was wondering if you had a recommendation of what sort of system of divination was compatible with DA, or if it was down to personal preference. I know LRM had specific suggestions for tarot decks due to the nature of the course...many thanks!

replies.title: Re: the dolmen arch
replies.content:
The Dolmen Arch dates from a time when divination wasn't yet included among the study programs of most occult groups. That was one of the great innovations of the Golden Dawn, and it wasn't until the 1940s that other traditions picked it up and ran with it, having noticed the very good results of divination training. That's why the DA, which I believe was originally written in the 1920s, doesn't reference it.

You can use any kind of divination you like with it. While I was working the Dolmen Arch system I practiced tarot, geomancy, astrology, Ogham, Coelbren, and the Sacred Geometry Oracle at various points, and I have no doubt that I could have also done just about anything else.

comments.title: Relating to the Welsh gods
comments.content:
I have been meditating on how to understand some of the Welsh gods on whom there is so little information. This is what I've come up with:

1. Elen of the Roads may not be the same soul as the fourth-century Welsh princess, but rather a divine soul willing to interact with humans through the interface of that legend. Humans relate to the gods through myth, and there is someone willing to answer when people respectfully call upon Elen through her story and symbolic correspondences.

2. This is how that college druid group in Minnesota wound up with a spiritual mentor. Someone divine or angelic noticed them adhering to the rules of magic, giving due diligence to ritual, and paying a lot of respect, and in turn offered to guide them.

3. This is why knowing next to nothing about Hu the Mighty isn't a prohibitive issue. (I have found that assuming his god form through "I am mighty!" and powering up has helped me to know him.)

4. Esus may not be the same Esus that guided the ancient druids, and that is okay. His presence to guide people doesn't lose any authenticity thereby.

5. This revival druid faith is Faustian in terms of the spiral staircase to Gwynfydd (no shade, I'm a big fan), and it may not survive the transition from the Piscean Age to the Aquarian. However, even if a sum total of only 200 souls ever reach Gwynfydd through this faith, it is still very much worth doing. It is worth building up egregores for those 200, and for ourselves in our future lives, should we go searching in the forest and yearning for nature-based spiritual truth.

Does this make sense to you, or have I gone off track? Thank you for any input you might have, and best wishes until February.

comments.title: A few astrological questions

I'm currently in the very beginning to delineate natal charts (mine and of a few others I know time and place of birth of). I guess it's still a long way to go and I have a few questions:

1) If a planet is very strongly placed in a chart - is it common that this planet "radiates" its influence into the complete chart regardless of its placing in the house and house rulership? In the example I have on my mind, Mars is extremely strong and almost every house seems to be under heavy martian influence.

2) The ascendant - at least as far I have read is quite often described as the "face" the people wears. If somebody is born around noon (or midnight), the ascendant is squared by the sun. Does this indicate some kind of conflict in the sense that, for example, there are either difficulties to truly express ones inner self to the outer world or that you "hide" yourself, the "face" being more like a mask? 3) Much more generally, I try to create analogies to better understand how astrology works or maybe more precisely what it is, that astrology describes. I imagine it as a subtle landscape - hills, valleys, etc. - that permanently changes its face. A hill can become a valley a valley can rise to a mountain, and so on. comments.title: How to learn to think and evaluate information/claims
comments.content:
Hey JMG, I had a question about thinking. Figured it was relevant to occultism since its training the mind.

What's the best way to learn to think well to truly evaluate arguments, and to compare information for different claims?

Arguments around race and gender come to mind. How do you truly educate yourself with a mind to being open and learning, but not merely absorbing wholesale current dogma without critical examination of if it fits the facts?

replies.title: Re: How to learn to think and evaluate information/claims
replies.content: Start by studying informal logic -- a good book on logical fallacies is a great place to start, since most of the current dogma depends on logical howlers. From there, go to books on the art of argumentation, especially those that focus on writing essays -- once you know how to construct an argument in written form, you also know how to take someone else's argument apart. Then study a controversy of the past, one where you have no emotional commitment to either side, and analyze the arguments. Then do the same thing to a controversy of the present, in which you do have an emotional commitment, and make yourself ignore your own feelings and analyze both sides' arguments dispassionately. That should do the trick.

replies.title: Re: How to learn to think and evaluate information/claims
replies.content: I stumbled across a great book on logical fallacies in my teens, the book was practically encyclopedic, listing a dozen variations on the red herring for instance, but sadly I can't remember the title. :(

First, this morning's Solstice ritual was one of the most absurd I've ever performed.

I began with the SIRP. Upon completing the Earth pentagram, I realized that I hadn't actually done the Opening ritual. What to do? At this point all 4 pentagrams were drawn and their energies pouring into the room, so I completed the ritual. I was unsure of what to do next and I had my geomantic wands out, so I said, "If I should start again from the beginning, let me cast a positive figure." I tossed the wands and Gwyn came up. So, I began from the beginning, without banishing. I had drawn 2 spirit pentagrams and started a third before I realized that I was singing the divine names in descending tones, as though I were banishing. So I started again, again. At this point, of course, the room was thick with currents of energy of various sorts flying all over the place.

The third time I made it all the way through the opening. Then a cat turned up-- a real one I mean, not an astral cat-- just prior to the meditation part of the ritual and left a colossal cat-poop in the next room, which somehow managed to overcome 2 sticks of incense. And then as I announced the watchword for the coming term, I realized that it could have rather negative as well as positive connotations; a potentially much better word popped into my head during the ritual, but I didn't go with it because I hadn't considered it beforehand.

All of this came after a morning which was ridiculous enough to begin with. My wife goes to work at 5, and I always wake up with her and make her food and coffee. Today I couldn't find any food, and I realized that the car was buried in snow and I didn't know where the ice scraper was. There followed an absurd adventure during which I spent 20 minutes digging out the car, realized she could just have taken our other car (which was free of snow), discovered that I had no time left to make her something to eat, managed to do so anyway, all the while we both kept losing random items like the food and the car keys and the coffee creamer. So I suspect that we may either have picked up a trickster spirit for the morning or else there were astrological factors messing with me.

comments.title: How To Save Christmas
comments.content:
I'm very fond of both Santa and Christmas, although I suspect that that is because I had a different experience of them from many of my fellow Americans. When I was growing up Advent was a rather austere season. It was cold and dark, but it slowly grew brighter as first the Advent wreath, then the decorations came out. Still, there were no cookies, parties or presents, and in fact we didn't even put up the tree until Christmas Eve! That day itself was largely a work day, during which we were set to various tasks, cleaning the house, icing cookies, stringing popcorn, and so forth. At the end of the day-- evening when I was very young, midnight later on-- we went to church. It was only after that that Christmas started. Music started up, lights went on, cookies and pastries were brought out, wine and beer flowed. And there began a Saturnalia that continued until New Years, during which the usual (quite strict, at least for the time) rules were relaxed, the house was filled with guests, and life took on the feeling of a weeklong party.

These being the post-Vatican II days, no one ever told us the reason for these things. It was only in adulthood that I learned that the Advent season was, indeed, a traditional fast, with the heaviest fast of all being on Christmas Eve, which was then broken at midnight after mass. Or that Christmas itself was always a multi-day feast, which followed the prolongued fast.

I'm sure you're aware of the various magical customs associated with the season. On St. Stephen's Day there is the sacrificing of the wren, and there are also old customs of blessing water and salt on this day, which are used both to bless homes and livestock. On St. John's Day (the 27th), wine is traditionally blessed and given to all members of the family, another protective ritual whose intention is found in the words of consecration: "Grant that by the merits of Saint John the Apostle and Evangelist, all who believe in You and drink of this cup may be blessed and protected." The day of the Holy Innocents follows, which is also associated with magical practices, including the odd custom of whipping the children in memory of Herod's murders-- and the odder custom of children whipping their parents. According to one source (https://www.sacred-texts.com/time/crt/index.htm):

In the Orlagau the girls on St. Stephen's, and the boys on St. John's Day beat their parents and godparents with green fir-branches, while the menservants beat their masters with rosemary sticks, saying:

“Fresh green! Long life!
Give me a bright thaler [or nuts, &c.].”

And there is more besides, all concluding with the feast of the Epiphany, during which more presents are traditionally given to commemorate the arrival of the Magi (or, the 3 astrologer-mages who used their knowledge of the Jupiter-Saturn conjunction to find the new world-savior). This is also the day in which chalk is consecrated and used in the blessing of homes.

All this is to say-- I agree very strongly with those who hate what Christmas has become in modern America. Mariah Carrey is bad enough; far worse is Justin Bieber's cover of Last Christmas, which I'm pretty sure is what they play on the radio all December in Hell. But there is a much deeper, richer heritage of magic, ritual and celebration associated with this season-- and no, it was most emphatically not all invented by Charles Dickens!

I wonder if, rather than simply banishing Santa Claus-- opposing force with force-- a better approach might be to reawaken an older version of Christmas, in which Santa was one of many parts. I described the Christmas tradition in my family as a Saturnalia, and I wonder if Saturn indeed might be part of the key to this. Saturn is the grim god of time, restriction, and austerity; the teacher who never spares the rod. But he is also the god of the Golden Age. Rather than simply banishing Santa, maybe we ought to invoke Saturn. Make advent an austere Saturnine fast, so that Christmas might be an equally saturnine feast-- the feast that only comes for those who can fast. All of this is tied together in the mystery of the Three Magi, who conclude the season with gifts of both gold, by which earthly pleasures may be purchased, and myrrh, for the anointing of the dead; these two are united in frankincense, which consecrates all things.

A ritual for the accomplishment of all of this might begin with an invocation of Saturn or Binah at the beginning of Advent, which would be followed with a proper fast for 4 weeks. Santa at his best and highest might be invoked on Christmas Eve-- although he could also be moved back to the 6th of December, to re-align his egregore with that of St. Nicholas. Other magical customs would be followed over the succeeding 12 days. All would conclude on Epiphany with an invocation of Melchior, Caspar and Balthazar; a final concluding 12th Night Party with the giving of presents; and finally the blessing of the house with consecrated Epiphany Chalk to seal in the season's blessings for the coming year.

What do you think?

replies.title: Re: How To Save Christmas
replies.content: I think it's an excellent idea. My guess is that Christmas turned toxic in America because we don't have those traditions and roots, and if you can just get back to the roots you'll be fine. We have a long road ahead of us before we find our own holidays and traditions!

comments.title: Re: Banishing Santa

This thread was getting very long up above, but it raised some major hackles with me and left me with some... questions and concerns... In doing this aren’t you putting yourself in opposition not only to a Christian Saint, but against Thor and Odin, against Jupiter against the personified spirit of charity and generosity, and against all Chesedic virtues... I.E. something very real, very powerful, and against something in need of balancing rather than exorcising... It strikes me like someone trying to get rid of their lust parasite by doing a full scale exorcism of Aphrodite... Or trying to address the worst evils of the church by exorcising Jesus from it.

It seems by attempting to completely exorcise Santa and all he stands for, vices and virtues alike (so generosity, charity, mercy and goodwill alongside greed and consumerism). You’d essentially come to embody the opposite, frugality yes, but pushed to its extreme towards miserliness... joy would transform to wrath and so on...

As I understand it, every god contains the entirety of the area of influence they govern and exists in some way on all levels... and they manifest in people’s lives often according to the spiritual state of those people... For some, Jesus has come to embody solar imbalances rather than solar virtues for instance since those people have approached him in an imbalanced way (but I’ve never seen you call for us to exorcise him, instead you’ve pointed out that these people seem to be worshipping different gods).

It strikes me that rather than Santa himself, the issue is that our society has come to him in an imbalanced way and begun to embody those imbalances. In fact... what we’ve got is Mercy pushed to its extreme and embodying the permitters of destruction... consuming is naturally something Fire does and is an essentially Martian act. I wouldn’t say Santa himself is a god of consumerism though any more than Jesus is the god of the Inquisition or Pan the literal Devil... Just that as with any god, if someone has corruption in their heart, the god will manifest in a corrupt way... Which says much more of that person than of the god...

Wouldn’t it make more sense to try to approach Santa in a more balanced way... perhaps by making prayers to Saint Nicholas and trying to empower him, by connecting to the older gods that wear the Santa archetype, by reaching into older stories including traditional folk tales and things like Tolkien’s letters from Father Christmas, or L. Frank Baum’s Life and Adventures of Santa Clause, and yes... by bringing in Krampus as a balancing element... comments.title: Druidry &c.
comments.content:
Blessed Alban Arthuan to you.
A couple of weeks ago, I worked down my pile of reading and came to "The Druidry Handbook" which I bought out of curiosity early this year. To my surprise and delight what jumped out at me was the realization that I've already been doing much of the tasks assigned to an First Degree Apprentice in the AODA as a matter of course over the past 25 years or so. Not everything in there, of course, but much of it, e.g. walking in the almost-wilderness of local parks at least an hour a day with my dog, and exploring geometry with compass and straight edge, massively reducing my energy footprint over the years and such.
I started formally doing Wicca rituals about 35 years ago and have been part of some covens that have come and gone over the years. replies.title: Re: Druidry &c.

1) The rituals given in The Druidry Handbook aren't AODA rituals; when it was published, we encouraged each initiate to create their own rituals for the seasons, and those were just examples. Salve, JMG. Thanks for always hosting these Monday sessions.

How do you regard The One of Neoplatonism? Some view it almost as proper entity, a sort of fountain from which all things spring, whereas other views have it as a perfectly simple first principle (The Good) that is not "prior" necessarily to the immortal gods, but that serves to differentiate them, almost through individuation. Is this a distinction without a difference, when it comes to praxis...?

A second question: Though there may be overlap between Hermeticism and Neoplatonism, it seems they may be two separate strands of esotericism that have become intertwined. Not sure which is prior in time, or again even if that matters, though it does seem that there may be differences in the systems.

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Well, to begin with, as a longtime student of Neoplatonism I start by realizing that what I think about the One is fairly irrelevant, as I'm way down the ladder of being and my speculations on that subject will be sharply limited by my own very small capacity to conceive the very great! For practical purposes, though, I tend to rely on the ideas of the late Pagan Neoplatonists, who held that the One is the principle through which all things, including the gods, come into being. It isn't their source, because that would imply flowing and change, and the One is beyond change; it is their principle, and all things are able to come into being because the One is. So it's not quite either of the choices you suggest.

2) They're different but compatible, having the same origin -- the creative fusion of Greek and Egyptian thought. The Hermetic literature was written in Egypt, and of course Plotinus was born, raised, and educated in Egypt, and was originally inspired by Ammonius Saccas, a Greek-Egyptian Platonist.

comments.title: CGD Exorcism Rough Draft
comments.content:
Hello again, JMG. Awhile back I asked about a CGD Greater Middle Pillar exercise https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/104945.html?thread=11522801#cmt11522801 . I'm up to doing it daily now.

I decided my next project is going to be adapting the exorcism ritual from Circles of Power to the CGD format. Given exorcism done badly can cause problems, I wanted to run my rough draft by you to make sure I didn't miss anything.

1) The CGD doesn't mention protective circles that I could find, so I've been studying the protective circle from diagram 11-1 (CoP page 223). I think the outer circle makes sense as is since the CGD uses both symbols in the same way, but the divine names in the inner circle need switching out. I came up with using the four elemental lords and ladies on the quarters (eg: east) and the four-letter names from the LRP between them starting with the south-east. Is there a better arrangement you'd recommend here?

Now for the actual ritual:

A - Have the standard Druid grade temple tools plus wand, sickle, and serpent's egg. Ensure you have deployed your protective method of choice. Having Asafoetida on hand is highly recommended as is filling the sacred well with consecrated holy water and using a strong blessing/purifying incense like frankincense in a block charcoal censer.

B - Open the temple in the Druid grade for a ceremony of exorcism, but the Ray of the Golden Dawn portion of the opening should use the intention of strengthening the magician for the work per the CGD conjuration rituals.

C - Call down the power of the Solar Ray into the sickle along the lines of the sickle consecration ritual.

D - Trace the summoning pentagram of spirit and vibrate the 9 names per the CGD wand/serpent's egg consecration ceremonies

E - Take the sickle in the right hand, raise it high, and use the CGD conjuration ritual's calling formula (H). Use the presence of HU the Mighty to "bind" a "phantom" rather than an elemental deity to "call" a "spirit".

F - Per CGD conjuration formula (J), have the phantom behold the sickle and instruct it to leave wherever we are removing it from, flee far away, and never return.

G - Proceed to the quarters, instructing the phantom to flee from each of the realms of the elements in the name of the lord or lady of the element and tracing the appropriate banishing pentagram with the tip of the sickle.

H - Declare the spirit duly exorcised.

I - Perform the OIW, calling down the light with all your strength.

J - Declare the ceremony of exorcism complete with due thanks and close the Druid temple in the usual way.

Questions:

2) Do I need to banish the phantom from the realm of Spirit? The CoP exorcism does, but the CGD sickle consecration doesn't address the realms of Spirit and I'm not sure if a troublesome phantom could even reach up that high.

3) Is calling down the current of force per (D) appropriate? The CoP ritual banishes by the names but the CGD sickle consecration is the only working tool consecration that omits the summoning pentagram + 9 names formula, so I wasn't sure.

4) Are there any other problems/omissions you can see?

5) What is the best way to practice a ritual of exorcism? Running around in search of nasties to banish seems like a bad plan regardless of skill level.

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: CGD Exorcism Rough Draft
replies.content:
1) That should work very well.

2) No. You don't have to worry about that; if I were writing Circles over again I wouldn't put that in.

3) Yes, and it's essential in an exorcism.

4) No, this looks good.

5) Perform it to purify your home of all noxious energies.

replies.title: Re: CGD Exorcism Rough Draft
replies.content: Per 2) is there any actively problematic effect of banishing Spirit in the CoP ritual or is it just cruft?

replies.title: Re: CGD Exorcism Rough Draft
replies.content: It's not problematic, just unnecessary.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG,

A question about the philosophy of divination. I've heard it said that divination doesn't predict the future, it reflects intent. Therefore, it seems that divination is more of a tool for inner exploration of a psychological and spiritual nature. It tells you what is happening within yourself, not what is happening "out there." Do you agree with this?

However, many people (I would say, even the majority) use divination for questions of a mundane nature, such as "Will I get the job?" or "Does she love me?" Assuming that these people are not misguided, and that their chosen oracle is returning valuable answers to them (meaning that "predictions" are actually coming true), the only possible solution seems to be that the intent revealed by the oracle has a mystical connection to events in the world that is not of a materialistic, physical nature - Carl Jung called this 'synchronicity.'

For example, if I ask my oracle of choice "Will I get the job?" the oracle is revealing my intent about the situation, which goes on to influence the events in a subtle way. Had I not asked the question, the intent would have influenced events without my knowledge. In Jungian terms, it's like the oracle is an inner radar that scans the contents of the unconscious and reveals the archetypes influencing my behavior from behind the scenes.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In my experience, the claim that divination is about intent rather than prediction is quite simply wrong. Divinations reliably warn of negative events -- things you don't want and don't intend -- some of which can be avoided, and some of which can't. If you ask "will I get the job" and you're any good, you should be able to expect an honest answer about whether you will get the job or not -- not a statement of your intent!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As an aside, the apparent belief that everything in the occult is "intent-side" heavy is wrong. Intent matters, surely, but some of this seems to be a focus on the microcosm to the utter exclusion of the macrocosm; this is my general problem with most New Thought, which seems to think "you can get whatever you want simply by thinking/willing it," regardless of what the forces outside "you" are saying. Have seen such an approach go off the rail in real life on several occasions.

Axé e Buon Natale Sole Invitto,
Fra' Lupo

comments.title: A Friend on a Troubling Path
comments.content:
I’m a regular commenter but I’m posting anonymously to protect the privacy of those involved... I have a dear friend who was, when I got to know them, a recovering Thelemite who had run hard into the Catholic Church after some really bad experiences in that wing of occultism and was occult friendly but pretty stringent in their Christianity...

A little over a year ago I had a dream that I was having a massive argument with this friend because I found out they’d been practicing the Sphere of Protection using Qlippothic demons as the god names invoked (I wrote it down in my journal and pretty much discounted it, since it was so out of character, but the dream stuck with me... mostly because the very thought of a ritual like that left me with such a deeply creeped out feeling...)

Anyway... this person has past-life memories of having repeatedly sold their soul to a malign entity and exhibits classic symptoms of this... a few months ago... actually probably close to the same time of that dream... they began drifting from the church again... a few months ago they started creating fictional stories involving demons and goetic magic, and then revealed that they were actively getting into Necromancy (since that’s such a loaded word these days that can range from practices that are borderline illegal to harmless ancestor veneration I pressed... but they were evasive)... a few days ago I discovered that they had begun actively worshipping Lucifer and had put up an altar to him...

I’m not entirely sure what to think of Lucifer, since he gets a Promethean liberating lord of light archetype projected onto him but is also a reservoir for Western Christendom’s entire collective shadow which includes some pretty nasty stuff... at any rate, measured against everything else that’s been building this seems like yet another bad sign... I did a reading on this and it implied this was likely a passing phase... but given past life history and the rest of the broad trend I’m still deeply concerned...

This is someone I love deeply and... I don’t -want- to just walk away from that friendship... I feel like I should confront them, as a concerned friend but I’ve been afraid to... I’ve completed a few correspondence courses in Neopagan groups prior to walking away from that scene and doing LRM (which I have finished, though I haven’t started Circles of Power), and I know a bit of natural magic, but this whole situation doesn’t feel in reach to address along those channels with my present experience...

Is there anything I can do? Is directly confronting this friend even a good idea? Or is this a time for prayer and distance for now? Or... am I overreacting to what’s likely just nothing? Can this friendship even be salvaged, or is this a time to just quietly slip away?

That said, your mileage may vary -- and again, this isn't my strong suit.

replies.title: Re: A Friend on a Troubling Path
replies.content:
After using JMG's Sacred Geometry Oracle, I think of the Vesica Piscis (the symbol used in the Venn Diagram) as a model for all interpersonal relationships.

There's your circle, which is you, the other circle, which is your friend, and the overlap, which is your friendship.

Lucifer clearly doesn't belong in your circle or the friendship. If you feel his influence in the overlap, you'll need to call it out and declare that it is unwelcome. You must assert your boundaries if that's the end of the friendship, then the friendship was already over.

Until then, you don't need to confront your friend. He has his circle and you have yours.

Regular banishings and protections will also repel toxic people and while it's cost me friendships, it's also attracted much better ones, so it's best to let it happen.

comments.title: Path of Occultist, Daily Etheric Tides
comments.content:
Dear J.M. Greer,

1) I've been pondering the nature of the three kinds of paths you've outlined before (mystic, mage, and occultist). Which books of yours are most specific to or distinctively about the path of the occultist (rather than the others)? What are some other books or sources you might recommend that most clearly deal with this as a distinct path from the others?

2) I'm interested in the daily etheric tides of solar and telluric currents, as well as their relationship with the daily etheric rhythms in the human body. I conjecture that it would be advantageous to synchronize certain magical and mundane daily activities with such tides. To understand this, I'm collecting daily observations of myself, local nature and animals, etc, correlated with local solar time and planetary hours (all this has grown naturally out of tracking the effects of practices). I expect daily tides will vary with the lunar and solar cycles, so I'm tracking those as well. For theoretical guidance, I'm using the Traditional Chinese Medicine and Daoist concept of the daily Qi cycle, the Indian/Ayurvedic concepts of Muhurtas and Dinacharya, and John Alden Knight's solunar theory and tables, used by hunters and fishermen.
Can you recommend any sources in Western occultism or traditions about this, and do you have any suggestions for such a project or investigation of this topic?

Thank you very much for taking time today for questions!
Regards,
Josh Rout

replies.title: Re: Path of Occultist, Daily Etheric Tides
replies.content:
1) My first significant book on the subject will be published next year. There's plenty of older material on the subject -- I'm partial to the books of Mouni Sadhu, Ernest Wood, and Geoffrey Hodson.

2) Those are among the things that got lost from the Western tradition in the wake of the Scientific Revolution, when the handful of people and lodges that kept the tradition going had to winnow things down to essentials. Most of what's been done in Western magical circles since then has been inspired by Asian traditions -- for example, the Golden Dawn used the cycles of tattvas from Hindu sources. See what you can work out, and have fun!

replies.title: Re: Path of Occultist, Daily Etheric Tides
replies.content: Thank you very much! I happened to come across Geoffrey Hodson's writings for the first time a couple of days ago, and I'm looking forward to your book.

comments.title: Wand Making
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

I have a question about wand making. You mention in your Celtic Golden Dawn book that one should make a wand out of wood with a yellow metal encircling one end and a white metal on the other. You said that although a wand could be very fancy (carved wood with gold and silver kind of stuff) it is also fine if it is just a wooden dowel with aluminum on one end and brass on the other. You say that the wood acts as an insulator while the metals conduct the energies that the mage is trying to work with. I notice that silver is the most electrically conductive metal and gold is the second most, while wood is obviously very poor at conducting electricity. This got me wondering if there is a connection between something's ability to conduct electrical and magical energy? If so, wouldn't silver be better than a mere base metal? And the same thing for gold versus something like copper? Likewise, could one wrap the wood with leather and have it still act as an insulator? Or is this taking the idea of conducting energy WAY to literally?

replies.title: Re: Wand Making
replies.content: The only way to find out is to experiment. I don't have the money for gold and silver, and used a dowel with one copper ring and one zinc ring. It worked very well.

comments.content:
1. You mentioned the Aquarian age began in 1879, there seems to be a lot of debate about when it started or will start, so how did you determine that date?

2. Can we expect the next 20 years of the Great Conjunction in Aquarius to be a microcosm of the Aquarian age?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) A number of late 19th and early 20th century occultists proposed that date. I noted that starting in 1879, a lot of things governed by Uranus (the planet ruling Aquarius) became far more prominent and central in public life, and a cascade of Uranian changes followed promptly. That seemed to indicate very clearly that the occultists in question were right.

2) No. The confusion between the era that began this afternoon and the Age of Aquarius is the product of sloppy astrology; the era that began in 1226 also had Jupiter and Saturn conjunct in the first degrees of Aquarius, for example. The era that started this year runs until 2219; the Age of Aquarius runs from 1879 t0 the year 4039 AD -- it's on a much bigger scale! While the current era will have some Aquarian characteristics, its inaugural chart has Uranus in his fall and retrograde, which does not bode well for the Aquarian current...

comments.title: Astrologers and Pluto Returns, Oh, My!
comments.content:
A shout out to Tunesmyth; thank you for the URL some time ago of the UK astrologer discussing the US Pluto Return and US craziness in general:
https://astrologyresearch.co.uk/pluto-return-anarchy-in-the-usa/

This is an interesting person! (I can't tell gender from the posts) According to other entries on their blog, they called both elections, 2016 and 2020, from astrological charts, with enough confidence to bet money on them and win.

I got curious about the U.S. Pluto Return in general and started doing some online searches. I found that the biggest stumbling block astrologers have with the U.S. natal chart is -- >what time of day< the U.S. came into being, due to the Continental Congress not recording times of day for events. Most astrologers settle on the Sibly Chart, for 5:10 pm on 4th July 1776 at Philadelphia, PA.

However, Dane Rudhyar made some interesting comments in his 1971 column -- http://khaldea.com/rudhyar/astroarticles/riddleusahoroscope.shtml.

The genesis of >the American people<, he said, can be reckoned as July 4, 1776, when we first declared to the world that we were our own nation. However, >the U.S.A. in its current form< may more rightly be reckoned as being born on March 4, 1789, at 12:01 am, New York City, when the current constitutional government went into effect. (http://www.khaldea.com/charts/usafed.shtml)

So this would mean we have >two< Pluto returns, one for the American people, and one for our Federal government.

I used an online astrology chart generator to figure out that the Pluto Return for the U.S. people is centered on February 10, 2022. (I used Philadelphia, PA because that's where it all started in 1776). If we reckon that things really started getting strange in mid-2019, that works out on the online chart-generation site to about 5 degrees of orb on Pluto's part. Another 5 degrees the other way puts us at about 10 February 2025 before the worst dies down.

The online chart site did not go far enough into the future for me to determine the Federal government's Pluto Return. It looks at a guesstimate like it will be centered sometime between 2036 to 2038.

- Cicada Grove
